data.title: Intel looks to benefit from rising demand in standard compute: Wedbush
data.description:
Quant Cloud Technology is reportedly seeing a surge in standard compute demand, which is being driven by Google (GOOG)(GOOGL), according to DigiTimes Asia.

If so, Wedbush analysts indicated this would prove more beneficial for Intel (INTC) rather than AMD (AMD).

"Even during our trip to Taiwan in early April, while general CPU/server demand was highlighted as seeing a continuation of the sustained growth realized in 2H'25, component vendors and ODMs we met with didn't call out a sudden expansion in demand," said Wedbush analysts Matt Bryson and Antoine Legault in an investor note.

"However, Quanta is apparently now talking to a surge in standard compute demand," they noted. "A lift in Q2 would also fit with our recent conversations with the supply chain, which suggest that secondary market pricing for CPUs has spiked in recent weeks, signaling a more severe constraint (vs. our previous conversation, which kept coming back to DRAM as the limiting factor for supply)."

The Taiwan-based Quanta is a global provider of data center hardware and infrastructure software.

"We see Intel as the primary beneficiary of this setup, given its ability to add capacity vs. AMD, which we believe should also benefit, but necessarily has less control over its output given its reliance on TSMC (TSM) wafers," Bryson added.

data.title: Acco Brands reiterates 2026 adjusted EPS of $0.84 to $0.89 as EPOS integration targets about $80M sales
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: ACCO Brands (ACCO) Q1 2026

Management view

  • "Last night, we reported first quarter results with sales and adjusted EPS above our outlook. We also reiterated full-year guidance," said (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford), adding that "first quarter consolidated sales grew 8%" and the company is "executing well on our key operational and strategic initiatives."

  • (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford) highlighted category and seasonal drivers, saying, "Sales for computer accessories within the segment were strong, reflecting new products and a meaningful end-user pipeline," and "in North America, early purchases of back-to-school products were better than anticipated." He added, "For the season, we are expecting back-to-school sales to be flat to up low single digits."

  • On portfolio strategy and EPOS, (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford) said, "Our overall strategy remains focused on expanding our product range in faster-growing categories with an emphasis on technology peripherals," and specified, "Our target for 2026 is for peripherals to grow to represent 25% of the company's projected revenue." He also announced, "As a result of the acquisition, Jeppe Dalberg-Larsen, President of EPOS, will now lead Technology peripherals for ACCO Brands."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) detailed acquisition accounting and integration milestones: "This $38 million gain represents the purchase price of EPOS compared to the preliminary fair market value of the business, which is primarily from working capital," and "our outlook includes $80 million of 2026 sales with a slightly higher gross profit rate than our consolidated average and neutral to adjusted EPS." She added, "We remain on track to deliver the outlined $15 million in cost synergies in 12 to 18 months," and "We recorded $7 million in restructuring charges, primarily related to this acquisition, most of which will be paid out in the next year."

Outlook

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) reaffirmed full-year guidance: "For 2026, we are reiterating our expectation for full year reported sales to be flat to up 3% and adjusted EPS to be within the range of $0.84 to $0.89," adding, "This outlook reflects a prudent sales expectation in the back half of the year given the global environment."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) gave cash flow, CapEx, restructuring, and leverage expectations: "Free cash flow is expected to be within the range of $75 million to $85 million, with approximately $25 million in restructuring payments and $15 million in CapEx," and "Lastly, we anticipate a consolidated leverage ratio within a range of 3.7x to 3.9x."

  • For Q2, (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) guided, "we expect reported sales to be up within a range of 1% to 4%" and "adjusted earnings per share to be within the range of $0.24 to $0.28," while noting "a lesser benefit from FX."

  • Versus last quarter’s messaging, management maintained the same full-year ranges previously issued, while adding a more explicit cost-risk framing tied to geopolitical conditions; (President, CEO & Director Thomas Tedford) said Q1 savings "may be offset by rising costs due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East" and the company "anticipate fuel costs and certain raw materials to increase globally with the impact weighted towards the back half of the year."

Financial results

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) reported, "Reported sales in the first quarter increased 8% with comparable sales down less than 3%," and said growth was "driven by FX and the EPOS acquisition."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) stated profitability metrics: "Gross profit for the first quarter was $107 million, an increase of 7%, with the margin rate of 31.1%, down 30 basis points," and "Adjusted operating income for the first quarter was $12 million, up $5 million versus the prior year."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) described segment performance: "In the Americas segment, sales were up 3% with comparable sales down 2%," while "In the International segment for the first quarter, sales were up 15%, with comparable sales down approximately 3%."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) reported balance sheet and cash flow items: "Free cash flow in the quarter was $1.4 million," "Inventory was up $67 million since the start of the year" (including "$27 million" related to EPOS), and "finished the quarter with a consolidated leverage ratio of 4.1x" with "approximately $252 million available for borrowing under our revolver."

Q&A

  • Gregory Burns, Sidoti & Company: asked why there was not more flow-through to full-year guidance after Q1 outperformance and requested the FX/acquisition contribution; (Executive VP & CFO OConnor) replied, "the first quarter for us is a pretty small quarter" and said EPOS is "about 5%" of full-year sales with FX "about 1%."

  • Gregory Burns, Sidoti & Company: asked about EPOS growth opportunities and why it had not expanded previously; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) answered, "We are early in the integration process with EPOS," added "we think there's opportunities... to offer a one-stop solution," and said, "I don't want to comment on the historical performance of EPOS."

  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital Markets: asked what drove EPOS outperformance vs expectations; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "we weren't really sure the uncertainty of an acquired business" and "we just found it prudent to be careful with our guidance assumptions for the business."

  • Joseph Gomes, NOBLE Capital Markets: asked for color on early back-to-school; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "we believe the season is going to be up modestly" and added, "we have good line of sight to the initial orders. They're at or better than our current forecast."

  • Kevin Steinke, Barrington Research: asked about Latin America go-to-market changes and gaming headwinds; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "We've adjusted our product assortment... incentive plans... and we've adjusted pricing," and on gaming said holiday was "relatively weak" creating retailer inventory pressure, while "our brand has taken share each month in the first 3 months of the quarter."

  • Kevin Steinke, Barrington Research: asked whether demand softening is already visible and about pricing; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) responded, "We haven't to date" and "We do have some planned price increases... and we'll continue to monitor the cost environment."

  • William Reuter, BofA Securities: asked about potential tariff refunds and oil/fuel cost headwinds; (Executive VP & CFO OConnor) said the claim is "somewhere in kind of the $25 million range" and "We don't expect anything in 2026," while (President, CEO & Director Tedford) added, "We've built our best thinking into our current guidance" given a "dynamic situation" tied to the Middle East conflict.

  • William Reuter, BofA Securities: asked what is included in the 25% peripherals goal and competitive dynamics; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said, "It consists of our brands, Kensington, PowerA, LucidSound and EPOS" and added, "We think those are large TAMs, growing TAMs and TAMs in which we have relatively small shares in."

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral, pressing on conservatism in guidance and external sensitivities; Burns asked, "given the strong first quarter, why wasn't there more flow-through," and Reuter challenged timing by noting "there are a lot of signs that indicate some refunds may be paid this year."

  • Management sentiment was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more cautious in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing prudence and uncertainty; (Executive VP & CFO OConnor) said, "with all the global uncertainty... we just prudently left... our guidance," and (President, CEO & Director Tedford) referenced "a dynamic situation" and said, "We've built our best thinking into our current guidance."

  • Compared with the prior quarter, management continued to project confidence in the strategy pivot but added more real-time cost inflation language; Q4 emphasized "stabilizing end markets," while Q1 highlighted that savings "may be offset" and that cost increases are "weighted towards the back half of the year."

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Guidance ranges were maintained quarter over quarter; Q1 reiterated "flat to up 3%" sales and adjusted EPS "$0.84 to $0.89," consistent with the prior quarter’s outlook.

  • Leadership participation shifted back to the CFO; Q4 noted "Deb O'Connor... will not be joined today," while Q1 included detailed CFO commentary on EPOS accounting, free cash flow, inventory, and leverage.

  • Strategy framing evolved from peripherals already being about a quarter of projected revenue in Q4 to a stated 2026 target; Q1 said, "Our target for 2026 is for peripherals to grow to represent 25% of the company's projected revenue."

  • Analyst focus remained concentrated on EPOS integration and guidance assumptions, with Q1 adding deeper probing on refunds and energy-driven costs; management more frequently emphasized prudence tied to geopolitical uncertainty than in Q4.

Risks and concerns

  • (President, CEO & Director Tedford) warned that cost savings "may be offset by rising costs due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East" and said fuel and certain raw material increases are expected "with the impact weighted towards the back half of the year," adding, "The company is carefully monitoring the situation and has taken appropriate steps to mitigate these potential impacts."

  • (Executive VP & CFO Deborah OConnor) cited working-capital and tariff-related pressures in inventory, saying inventory was up due to EPOS, "seasonal inventory build and higher tariff costs."

  • Gaming demand risk was framed as industry and consumer-driven; (President, CEO & Director Tedford) said the market faced "broad industry challenges and softer consumer spending" and that holiday weakness created "inventory opportunities for retailers" that affected Q1.

Final takeaway

ACCO Brands’ management emphasized a Q1 start that came in above its internal outlook while holding 2026 guidance steady, citing a smaller Q1 profit mix and uncertainty around back-half demand and cost inflation. The company positioned EPOS as a key portfolio move toward technology peripherals, expecting about $80 million of 2026 sales over 11 months, $15 million of cost synergies in 12 to 18 months, and a modest profit contribution, while flagging fuel/raw-material and tariff-related variables as swing factors for the remainder of the year.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Allied Gold falls again as Mali conflict futher escalates
data.description:
Allied Gold (AAUC), which is being acquired by China's Zijin Mining (ZIJMF) (ZIJMY), dropped again, falling 1.5%, as a conflict in Mali appeared to intensify. 

Al-Qaeda-led militants are closing in on Mali's capital and forcing a retreat of the Russian mercenaries who were trying to stop the spread of the Islamist group across the country, according to a  WSJ report on Friday. In the capital Bamako, militants imposed a siege, attacked the airport and killed the defense minister. 

Allied Gold (AAUC) shares dropped 4.5% on Monday after the defense minister was killed in a suicide attack by an al-Qaeda affiliate on Saturday.

In the wake of the latest fighting, Allied Gold (AAUC) on Tuesday confirmed the normal course conduct of business  at its operating mines in Mali. 

"In light of recent events in Mali involving the conflicts between government and insurgent groups, the Company also wishes to report that while its business operations in Mali continue in the normal course, with such activities occurring at far distances from the Company’s operations, the Company continues to monitor the situation and take precautions to ensure the safety and wellbeing of persons employed by the Company in the country, Allied Gold (AAUC) said in a statement on Tuesday.

Allied Gold didn't immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's email request for comment on Friday.Zijin Mining (ZIJMF) (ZIJMY) agreed to buy Allied Gold (AAUC) for ~C$5.5B (US$4B) in cash in January.

data.title: Intel granted early termination from FTC for SambaNova Systems partnership
data.description:
• Intel (INTC) was granted an early termination notice on its pending SambaNova Systems partnership, the Federal Trade Commission said on Friday.
  • Santa Clara, Calif.-based Intel said in February that it was taking part in a $350M funding round for the AI chip startup and would also form a partnership with the company.
  • SambaNova Systems raised $350M in Series E financing to expand manufacturing and cloud capacity, with new investors including Vista Equity Partners, Cambium Capital, Intel Capital, Battery Ventures, and accounts advised by T. Rowe Price Associates.
  • The AI hardware and software company said SoftBank (SFTBY) will be the first customer to deploy SN50 within its next‑generation AI data centers in Japan. The SN50 delivers five times more compute per accelerator and four times more network bandwidth than the previous generation, the company added. Intel CEO Lip-Bu Tan also serves as SambaNova's executive chairman. 
  • Intel had previously discussed buying the startup for about $1.6B, including debt.

data.title: Google, Nvidia among 8 inking deals with Pentagon for AI use on classified work
data.description:
The U.S. Department of War has entered into agreements with eight technology companies to use their AI capabilities in classified settings, boosting the Pentagon's efforts to get access to cutting-edge AI.

The Department of War, also known as the Department of Defense, inked agreements with ChatGPT maker OpenAI (OPENAI); Alphabet’s (GOOG) (GOOGL) Google; Elon Musk’s SpaceX (SPACE), which owns xAI (X.AI), the maker of AI chatbot Grok; Microsoft (MSFT); Amazon's (AMZN) Amazon Web Services; Nvidia (NVDA); and startup Reflection AI to deploy their advanced AI capabilities on the department's classified networks for lawful operational use.

Following this announcement, the Undersecretary of War for Research and Engineering and Chief Technology Officer Emil Michael noted that Oracle (ORCL) has joined the efforts.

"Following this morning’s announcement, the @DeptofWar is announcing that Oracle has officially agreed to join the list of AI companies deploying frontier capabilities on the Department’s classified networks," said Michael on X.

Shares of Oracle (ORCL) jumped about 7% on Friday.

The DoD said that SpaceX, OpenAI, Google, Nvidia, Reflection, Microsoft, and AWS will provide resources to deploy their capabilities for both Impact Level 6 (IL6) and Impact Level 7 (IL7) environments.

The agency noted that the effort supports its AI Acceleration Strategy by enabling new capabilities across its three core tenets of warfighting, intelligence, and enterprise operations.

GenAI.mil, the department's official AI platform, is already demonstrating the scale and impact of this acceleration. Over 1.3M department personnel have used the platform, according to DoD.

The Department of War declined to comment further but directed Seeking Alpha to a post by Michael on X. "Today, the @DeptofWar entered into agreements with SEVEN of the world's leading frontier AI model and infrastructure companies to deploy frontier capabilities on the Department's classified networks."

"This is just the latest initiative in our mandate to create an AI-FIRST WAR DEPARTMENT," Michael added.

"For more than a decade, AWS has been committed to supporting our nation's military and ensuring that our warfighters and defense partners have access to the best technology at the best value. We look forward to continuing to support the Department of War's modernization efforts, building AI solutions that help them accomplish their critical missions," said AWS spokesperson Tim Barrett in an email to Seeking Alpha.

Google declined to comment beyond the Department's press release in an email to Seeking Alpha. Nvidia declined to comment as well.

Oracle, SpaceX, OpenAI, Reflection, and Microsoft did not immediately respond to Seeking Alpha's request for comment.

Michael, speaking to CNBC, said that the Department will look at how to work with all the models in the coming year. He added that they cannot rely on only one partner for AI.

The deals show how much of Silicon Valley is agreeing to the DoD's terms in a way that Anthropic (ANTHRO) did not. The Claude AI chatbot maker — which is backed by Amazon (AMZN) and Google — has been in a legal tussle with the Pentagon. Anthropic had sued the DoD after the department dropped its contract with the company and labeled it a supply chain risk. The company had rejected the Department of War's demand for unrestricted access to its AI models.

However, recently, after Anthropic unveiled its most powerful model, Claude Mythos Preview, a general-purpose frontier model, which it called its "most capable yet for coding and agentic tasks," the Trump Administration seemed to have warmed up a bit. However, the tussle continues.

Michael also told CNBC that Mythos is a separate issue, adding that Mythos is being dealt with government-wide.

Some companies, including OpenAI and SpaceX, had had initial agreements with the Department of War. Earlier this week, it was reported that Google signed a deal allowing the Pentagon to use Google’s AI models on classified work.

Michael said in a statement to the Wall Street Journal that, “We are equipping the warfighter with a suite of AI tools to maintain an unfair advantage and achieve absolute decision superiority."

For many months, Anthropic’s Claude models were some of the only tools available in classified settings through Palantir Technologies (PLTR), which offers AI services to the military through its platform Maven, the report added.

Update: The story was updated with the Department of War CTO's post regarding Oracle joining the AI initiative.

data.title: Stocks rise to fresh record highs as May trading starts with oil falling
data.description:
The stock market advanced to a new intraday record high Friday as Wall Street looked to kick off the month of May on an optimistic note, with oil prices retreating and corporate earnings going strong. 

The S&P 500 (SP500) rose 0.8% after closing above the 7.2K level for the first time ever in the prior session, the Dow (DJI)  increased 0.2%, and the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) -- crossing the 25K mark for the first time—gained 1.1%.

U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude futures (CL1:COM) gapped down 2.9% to $102 a barrel after Iran reportedly submitted its response via Pakistani mediators to the latest U.S. amendments to a draft deal to end the Middle East war. International benchmark Brent crude futures (CO1:COM) also dipped 1.7% to $108.53 a barrel, as the news revived hopes for an agreement to end the conflict.

Meanwhile, Apple (AAPL) was in the spotlight, with its shares advancing 4.5% after the technology giant turned in better-than-expected results for fiscal Q2, as the iPhone 17 line continued to impress consumers.

Investors were also assessing fresh reads on U.S. manufacturing, with April S&P U.S. Manufacturing PMI rising slightly more than expected from a month earlier. The separate U.S. ISM Manufacturing PMI was unchanged in April and fell short of the consensus, with several components of the measure pointing to a stagflationary environment.

U.S. Treasury yields were mixed across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was roughly flat at 4.38%, while the 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) rose 2 basis points to 3.89%. The 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) was little changed at 4.97%.

data.title: FOMC's Hammack, Logan think forward guidance should not have implied easing bias at this time
data.description:
Federal Open Market Committee's voting members, Beth Hammack and Lorie Logan, do not think the forward guidance should have implied a bias towards a rate cut at this time.

Cleveland Fed President Hammack and Dallas Fed President Logan supported maintaining the target range for the federal funds rate at the April FOMC meeting. However, they opposed the inclusion of an easing bias in the post-meeting statement.

The statement said, "In considering the extent and timing of additional adjustments to the target range for the federal funds rate, the committee will carefully assess incoming data, the evolving outlook, and the balance of risks."

While the statement doesn't explicitly say the next rate move would lean toward a cut, the use of the term "additional adjustments" could imply a reduction since the last adjustment was a cut.

"I disagree with that assessment of the policy outlook," said Lorie Logan. "I am increasingly concerned about how long it will take inflation to return all the way to the FOMC's 2% target."

"Even before recent increases in the prices of energy and other commodities, those measures had been running meaningfully above 2%, leaving doubts about how long it will take inflation to return to target," said the FOMC voting committee member.

"The conflict in the Middle East raises the prospect of prolonged or repeated supply disruptions that could create further inflationary pressures. At the same time, the labor market has been stable, with low unemployment and payroll job gains keeping pace with labor force growth," said Logan.

According to Logan, it could plausibly be appropriate for the FOMC's next rate change to be either an increase or a cut. Meanwhile, Hammack sees this "clear easing bias" in the statement as no longer appropriate given the outlook.

"Uncertainty around the economic outlook has increased in 2026 and makes the future path for monetary policy more uncertain as well," said Hammack, adding there are upside risks to inflation and downside risks to growth and employment.

data.title: NPK raises 2026 outlook to $310M-$325M revenue as it plans $40M-$45M capex for ~50% capacity expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NPK International Inc. (NPKI) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Matthew Lanigan (President, CEO & Director) said the company had a “strong start to 2026” and reported “total rental and service revenues setting another quarterly record at $52 million,” alongside “$23 million” of product sales revenue and “$22 million adjusted EBITDA.”
  • Lanigan highlighted cash and capital actions in the quarter: “$21 million of cash flow from operations and $5 million of free cash flow while also expanding our rental fleet by 4%, repaying our revolving credit facility and using $3 million to fund share repurchases.”
  • Lanigan said the board approved a manufacturing capacity expansion: “increase our production capacity by approximately 50% from current levels,” with planned investment “$40 million to $45 million over the next 5 quarters” and “the goal of bringing the additional capacity online by mid-2027.”
  • Gregg Piontek (Senior VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer) summarized the quarter’s mix and the Grassform impact: “Rental revenues grew 27% year-over-year, reflecting 12% organic growth, combined with a $4 million contribution from the Grassform acquisition.”

Outlook

  • Piontek raised full-year guidance: “we have raised the range of our full year 2026 outlook, now anticipating total revenues of $310 million to $325 million and adjusted EBITDA of $92 million to $102 million,” adding, “product sales remained relatively in line with 2025 levels.”
  • On Q2, Piontek said, “we expect to deliver 20% year-over-year growth in rental and service revenues in Q2,” while “Q2 revenues will be fairly in line with prior Q2 levels” for product sales and “Q2 gross margin is also expected to be roughly in line with the prior Q2 results.”
  • Versus the prior quarter’s call, management added specific manufacturing expansion spending to 2026 CapEx expectations, with Piontek now guiding “total net CapEx of $75 million to $90 million for the year, including $30 million to $35 million of current year spending for the manufacturing expansion project.”

Financial results

  • Piontek reported first-quarter profitability and taxes: “The first quarter gross margin was 36.2%,” “Income tax expense was $3.6 million… an effective tax rate of 26%,” and “Adjusted EPS from continuing operations was $0.12 per diluted share.”
  • On costs, Piontek attributed margin pressure mainly to timing and utilization: “The modest sequential gross margin compression primarily reflects the effect of lower rental fleet utilization early in the quarter attributable to the timing of large-scale projects.”
  • Piontek reported cash flow and balance sheet details: “Operating activities generated $21 million,” “Net CapEx used $16 million,” and the company ended with “total debt of $11 million and total cash of $7 million for a net-debt position of $4 million,” plus “$148 million of availability under our bank facility.”

Q&A

  • Aaron Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: asked about greenfield vs. brownfield and high-voltage project timing; Matthew Lanigan (President, CEO & Director) said, “we’re not really — it’s still a little early for some of the larger, higher voltage projects,” and added the pipeline “remains as robust as where we left it last quarter.”
  • Spychalla, Craig-Hallum: asked how long the capacity expansion supports growth and options to add more; Lanigan said, “we see this plant giving us plenty of capacity through the end of the decade,” and noted “plenty of room in our Louisiana facility.”
  • Laura Maher, B. Riley: asked if returns change with higher CapEx; Gregg Piontek (Senior VP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer) said, “I would expect no change in the overall expectation.”
  • Maher, B. Riley: asked magnitude and durability of pricing; Lanigan said, “I’d probably frame it in low single digit,” and added, “we would expect to be able to hold that and maybe add to that moving forward in the year.”
  • Min Cho, Texas Capital Securities: asked if capacity supports both rentals and product sales; Lanigan said, “we feel comfortable that we can meet both,” and cited cross-rental helping “with the price of diesel now kind of rising with the conflict in the Middle East.”
  • Cho, Texas Capital: asked about cadence and CapEx timing; Piontek said CapEx has “some front-loaded elements,” and added, “Q3 typically pulls back a bit from Q2 and then rebounds and surges… in Q4.”
  • Brandon Rogers, ROTH: asked composite penetration and pace; Lanigan said, “We still see roughly 1/4 of the market in total being composite.”
  • Rogers, ROTH: asked risks to mid-2027 timeline and $40 million to $45 million budget; Lanigan said, “we feel pretty good that… we’re going to be able to deliver this within the time frame and the budget.”
  • William Dezellem, Tieton: asked if high-voltage work implies faster 2027 growth; Lanigan replied, “you would logically say, yes,” while adding, “A little early to piece it all together.”
  • Dezellem, Tieton: asked Grassform integration timing and acquisition criteria; Lanigan said integration would be “within the next sort of 3 to 6 months,” and that future deals would target places “where we can accelerate composite market share relative to timber incumbent.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was neutral to slightly positive, focusing on durability of the pipeline, pricing, capacity constraints, and acquisition cadence; questions repeatedly pressed on project timing and what could change the mid-2027 expansion plan.
  • Management’s tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks (Lanigan: “We are very pleased with our strong start to 2026”) and largely steady in Q&A, while occasionally cautious on timing (Lanigan: “it’s still a little early for some of the larger, higher voltage projects”).
  • Versus the prior quarter, management language shifted from “we will provide more details” on expansion (Q4) to a board-approved plan with spending and timing (Q1), while analyst focus stayed centered on visibility, phasing, and capacity/rental dynamics.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • The biggest strategic update was moving from evaluation to approval of a manufacturing expansion; in Q4, Lanigan said the team was “wrapping up the evaluation of manufacturing expansion options,” while in Q1 he said the board “approved our plans” for ~50% capacity growth by mid-2027.
  • Guidance tightened upward at the midpoint: Q4’s full-year outlook was “total revenues of $305 million to $325 million and adjusted EBITDA of $88 million to $100 million,” while Q1 raised this to “$310 million to $325 million” and “$92 million to $102 million.”
  • The CapEx framework broadened to include the expansion in the annual range; Q4 guided “net CapEx of $45 million to $55 million” excluding the expansion, while Q1 guided “total net CapEx of $75 million to $90 million” including “$30 million to $35 million” for the expansion in 2026.

Risks and concerns

  • Margin sensitivity to project timing and utilization remained explicit: Piontek tied Q1 margin compression to “lower rental fleet utilization early in the quarter attributable to the timing of large-scale projects.”
  • Lanigan flagged monitoring of supply chains and input costs: “we continue to monitor” Middle East conflict impacts and “have not seen any meaningful impacts to date,” while emphasizing diversified sourcing and commercial flexibility to “pass through impacts where practical.”
  • Execution risk around systems and integration was discussed as ongoing work: Lanigan referenced “the significant transition to our new ERP system implemented in the first quarter,” and said Grassform integration should be “within the next sort of 3 to 6 months.”

Final takeaway

Management framed Q1 as record rental-and-service revenue with raised full-year revenue and adjusted EBITDA guidance, alongside a board-approved, multi-quarter manufacturing capacity expansion plan targeting ~50% more output by mid-2027. The call emphasized continued utility-driven demand, planned fleet growth, and a capital allocation mix that prioritizes growth CapEx while maintaining share repurchases and keeping acquisition criteria focused on accelerating composite share gains versus timber incumbents.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Shentel targets 510,000 Glo Fiber passings by 2026 while aiming for positive free cash flow in 2027
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Shenandoah Telecommunications Company (SHEN) Q1 2026

Management view

  • Edward McKay (President & CEO) reported fiber expansion execution and customer growth, saying, “During the quarter, we released 22,000 passings to sales, bringing our total Glo Fiber expansion markets passings to 449,000,” and “We added approximately 6,000 Glo Fiber net customers in the first quarter... and we now serve a total of 94,000 customers.”

  • Edward McKay (President & CEO) highlighted Commercial Fiber demand and construction timing, saying, “Our Commercial Fiber business also delivered a strong quarter with 196,000 in sales bookings and revenue growth of 4.7% year-over-year,” and “We remain on track to complete our Glo Fiber expansion in 2026, reaching 510,000 passings.”

  • Edward McKay (President & CEO) emphasized penetration, churn, and mix, including “penetration rose to 20.9%,” “our average monthly churn was 0.92%,” and that “nearly 82% of our new residential customers in the first quarter, [selected] speeds of 1 gig or higher.”

  • James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) said, “Revenues grew 4.8% to $92.2 million,” and “Adjusted EBITDA grew $4.1 million or 15% to $31.7 million.”

Outlook

  • James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) said, “We reiterate our annual guidance for 2026,” and “We expect revenues of $370 million to $377 million, adjusted EBITDA of $131 million to $136 million and CapEx net of grant reimbursements to be $220 million to $250 million.”

  • Management framed 2027 free cash flow as tied to construction completion and capital intensity: James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) said the company has “3 catalysts converging that we expect will lead us to generating and growing positive free cash flow in 2027 and beyond,” listing “declining capital intensity as we exit the construction phase of our business plan” and “declining cost of capital after refinancing our debt in 2025.”

  • Compared with the prior quarter, guidance figures and the 2027 free cash flow timeline were reiterated, while Q1 commentary added that “all planned Glo Fiber markets have now been launched,” shifting emphasis toward adding passings inside existing launched markets.

Financial results

  • James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) attributed revenue drivers by segment: Glo Fiber expansion market revenue growth was “$6.4 million or 34.6%,” Commercial Fiber revenue grew “$900,000 or 4.7%,” while incumbent broadband markets revenue declined “$2.2 million,” and RLEC revenues declined “$800,000.”

  • James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) described margin drivers, saying, “Adjusted EBITDA margins increased 300 basis points to 34.4%,” driven by “high incremental margins in Glo Fiber, fewer lower-margin video customers and a favorable true-up related to a government grant.”

  • Capital spending and grants were detailed by James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO): “We invested $75.8 million in capital expenditures in the first quarter 2026 and collected $11.5 million in government grants for net CapEx of $64.3 million,” and “We have also completed construction of 88% of our target Glo Fiber passings as of March 31.”

  • Liquidity and leverage were updated by James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO): “As of March 31, we had $707 million in outstanding debt and $636 million of net debt,” “We have no debt maturities until 2029,” and “Total available liquidity was approximately $195 million.”

Q&A

  • Hamed Khorsand, BWS Financial Inc.: asked if competition is affecting subscriber additions; Edward McKay (President & CEO) said, “In our Glo Fiber markets, we’re not,” adding incumbent churn was “a little bit of churn to Starlink with some of the promotional offers they launched in the first quarter.”

  • Hamed Khorsand, BWS Financial Inc.: asked whether marketing expense will rise as construction winds down; Edward McKay (President & CEO) answered, “I would expect marketing expense to be similar and the primary impact will be the decline in CapEx.”

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: asked about sustainability of higher-speed mix and ARPU trajectory; Edward McKay (President & CEO) said, “we do think that’s sustainable going forward,” and “longer term, I think there’s opportunity there.”

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: asked about Commercial Fiber growth objectives and data center opportunity; James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) said the company is “generally expecting mid-single-digit revenue growth rates from the commercial business,” and later added, “there is about 20 data centers being either built or being built close to our fiber in the 8 states that we operate in,” while calling revenue expectations “a little premature.”

  • Vikash Harlalka, New Street Research LLP: asked about industry pricing power and deflation risk; Edward McKay (President & CEO) said, “in our Glo Fiber business, we’re expecting fairly flat ARPU in the near term,” while noting incumbent ARPU saw “a slight decline.”

  • Vikash Harlalka, New Street Research LLP: asked whether Starlink’s impact was adds or churn and how Shentel will respond; Edward McKay (President & CEO) cited a Starlink offer of “$15 off for 4 months” and “free equipment,” and said Shentel “increased speeds significantly in our rural incumbent areas” to “help mitigate.”

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum Capital Group LLC: asked for clarity on competing with Starlink given fiber speed advantages; Edward McKay (President & CEO) said, “we have the ability to offer gigabit speeds,” and added that after the discount expires, Shentel is “favorable from a pricing standpoint and a speed standpoint.”

Sentiment analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral on industry pricing and competition, reflected in concerns such as “pricing power” and follow-ups probing Starlink’s impact and sustainability of higher-speed demand.

  • Management: slightly positive in prepared remarks (execution and cadence) and more explanatory in Q&A; Edward McKay (President & CEO) acknowledged mixed pricing dynamics (“fairly flat ARPU in the near term”) while positioning mitigation steps on churn and value.

  • Versus last quarter, the analyst tone shifted from 5-year price guarantee and build-plan questions toward a broader industry concern on “deflation” and a more pointed focus on Starlink promotions.

Quarter-over-quarter comparison

  • Q1 emphasized operational momentum and launch completion (“all planned Glo Fiber markets have now been launched”) while Q4 emphasized portfolio mix shift and structural actions, including a “workforce reduction of approximately 10% of our employees” and a decision “to pass on investments in certain Ohio markets” due to “rising aerial make-ready costs.”

  • Q1 added a new competitive theme in rural incumbent areas, attributing higher churn to “promotional activity from satellite competition,” while Q4’s competitive discussion centered on the rationale and expected ARPU impact of the 5-year price guarantee.

  • Management’s guidance posture remained consistent (reiterated 2026 ranges and the 2027 free cash flow inflection), while Q1 provided more detail on construction progress (88% of target Glo Fiber passings completed as of March 31).

Risks and concerns

  • Edward McKay (President & CEO) cited churn pressure in rural incumbent markets tied to “promotional activity from satellite competition,” with mitigation including speed increases “at the same price to better differentiate our service.”

  • James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) characterized Commercial Fiber as “a little bit of a lumpy business,” noting larger deals “tends to be a little lumpy,” while pointing to continued enterprise additions.

  • Liquidity and leverage were highlighted as context for funding the plan: James Volk (Senior VP of Finance & CFO) stated “$707 million in outstanding debt,” “no debt maturities until 2029,” and “approximately $195 million” of available liquidity.

Final takeaway

Management described Q1 as continued fiber-led execution: higher Glo Fiber passings released to sales, customer growth, and stable-to-flat near-term ARPU expectations in Glo Fiber, while addressing rural incumbent churn tied to Starlink promotions with speed increases. The company reiterated full-year 2026 guidance ranges, emphasized that the Glo Fiber expansion remains on track to finish by the end of 2026, and continued to frame 2027 as the planned inflection to positive free cash flow supported by adjusted EBITDA growth, lower capital intensity, and a lower cost of capital following refinancing.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Headwater Exploration Non-GAAP EPS of $0.41, revenue of $169.9M
data.description:
• Headwater Exploration press release (HWX:CA): Q1 AFFO  of $0.41.
  • Revenue of $169.9M.
  • Achieved an operating netback inclusive of financial derivatives (2) of $49.90/boe and an adjusted funds flow netback (2) of $45.20/boe.
  • Achieved net income of $35.6 million ($0.15 per share basic) equating to $16.51/boe.
  • Executed a $66.7 million capital expenditure (3) program inclusive of development, exploration and secondary recovery implementation.
  • Declared a cash dividend of $26.2 million, or $0.11 per common share. To date, Headwater has paid out cumulative dividends of $343.7 million to shareholders ($1.45 per common share).
  • As at March 31, 2026, Headwater had adjusted working capital (1) of $2.4 million and no outstanding bank debt.
